1: Psalm 106 opens with the same Hallĕlû-yāh that closed Ps 105, binding the two psalms into a deliberate diptych. The matched structure: Ps 105 surveys YHWH's gracious acts in history; Ps 106 surveys Israel's repeated failures within that same history. Both use the same historical tradition (Exodus through the settlement period) but with opposite emphases. Ps 106 is a national confession of sin in the form of a historical survey — a genre similar to Nehemiah 9:5-37 and Daniel 9:4-19, which also recall the pattern of sin-and-grace through Israel's history. "His steadfast love (ḥesed) endures forever" — the liturgical response formula from Ps 118 and 136, placed here in the opening call to praise before the catalogue of failures. The confession can only be made because the answer is already known: ḥesed endures.
2: "Who can proclaim the mighty acts (gĕbûrôt) of the LORD or fully declare his praise?" — the rhetorical question acknowledges the inadequacy of any recitation to capture the fullness of divine acts. Gĕbûrôt (mighty deeds, heroic acts) is the vocabulary of divine warfare and power (cf. Psalms 145:4; Psalms 150:2; Job 26:14). The question is not a denial that praise is possible but a confession that it is always incomplete. In the context of a psalm that will catalog Israel's failures, this opening acknowledgment of YHWH's incomprehensible power creates the contrast: Israel's faithlessness is set against a background of inexhaustible divine greatness that even praise cannot fully encompass.
3: "Blessed are those who act justly (šōmĕrê mišpāṭ), who always do what is right (ʿōśēh ṣĕdāqāh bĕkol-ʿēt)" — a wisdom beatitude (ʾašrê) placed at the opening of a confession psalm. The beatitude defines what the confessors should have been — the standard against which the failures of Psalms 106:6-46 are measured. "Always do what is right" (bĕkol-ʿēt = in every time/season) sets an absolute consistency that the historical survey will show Israel consistently failed to maintain. The function of the beatitude here is to set the standard before the confession begins — making the depth of the failure clear by contrast.
4: "Remember me, O LORD, when you show favor to your people; come to my aid when you save them" — the first-person petition embedded in a communal psalm signals that this is not merely historical recitation but personal intercession. The individual speaker identifies with the community of Israel in both its past failures and its future hope of restoration. "When you show favor (birṣôn)" — rāṣôn (favor, acceptance, delight) is the covenantal term for the moment of divine acceptance, used of acceptable sacrifices (Leviticus 1:3) and of the year of YHWH's favor (Isaiah 61:2, which Jesus reads in Luke 4:19). The prayer is that the individual be included in the community's restoration.
5: "That I may enjoy the prosperity of your chosen ones, that I may share in the joy of your nation, and join your inheritance in giving praise" — three desired participations: the prosperity (ṭôbāh) of the chosen, the joy (śimḥāh) of the nation, the praise of the inheritance (naḥălāh). The "inheritance" here refers both to the land and to the covenantal identity of the people — the whole complex of relationship, land, and worship that constitutes Israel's life before YHWH. The prayer orients toward the future restoration that the psalm will build toward — after the confession of past failure comes the hope of future inclusion in the covenant community's restoration.
6: "We have sinned, even as our ancestors did; we have done wrong and acted wickedly" — the confession proper begins (Psalms 106:6-46). The phrase "even as our ancestors did (kĕʾăbôtênû)" is the key move: the present generation identifies with past generations' sins. This is the solidarity-in-sin that characterizes the great OT confessions (Nehemiah 9:16-18; Daniel 9:4-8; Ezra 9:6-7). The three-verb climax — "sinned (ḥāṭāʾnû), done wrong (heʿăwînû), acted wickedly (hiršaʿnû)" — covers the three categories of transgression in Hebrew moral theology: missing the mark, bending/distorting, and acting wickedly/criminally. The full range of moral failure is owned without qualification.
7: "When our ancestors were in Egypt, they did not grasp the meaning of your wonders; they did not remember your many acts of faithful love, and they rebelled by the sea, the Red Sea" — the first failure: failure to understand the plagues and Exodus. "Did not grasp (lōʾ hiskîlû)" — the verb is the wisdom term for discernment and understanding (Proverbs 1:3; Daniel 1:4). The plagues were signs with interpretive content, but the people did not understand their theological significance. The rebellion at the sea (Exodus 14:10-12 — the people said "Was it because there were no graves in Egypt that you brought us to the desert to die?") is the paradigmatic failure of faith at the very moment of greatest divine action.
8: "Yet he saved them for his name's sake, to make his mighty power known" — the divine motivation for rescue despite failure: "for his name's sake (lĕmaʿan šĕmô)." The "name" (šēm) in covenantal theology is YHWH's reputation and self-disclosure — his name is at stake when his covenant people are at risk. Ezekiel develops this most explicitly (Ezekiel 20:9, 14, 22; Psalms 36:21-23): YHWH repeatedly says he acted "for the sake of my name, so that it would not be profaned in the sight of the nations." The salvation serves the purpose of demonstrating divine power and character — even the failure of the people cannot stop YHWH from fulfilling his name's commitments.
9: "He rebuked the Red Sea, and it dried up; he led them through the depths as through a desert" — the sea crossing described as a divine rebuke (wayyigeʿar). YHWH "rebukes" the sea as a superior authority commands an inferior — the same verb used for rebuking demonic forces in the NT (Matthew 8:26; Psalms 17:18; Mark 1:25). The drying up of the sea (wĕyeḥĕrab) echoes Gen 1 (separation of waters from dry land) and Exodus 14:21 (the east wind drying the sea floor). "Through the depths (tĕhômôt) as through a desert" — the cosmic deep water (Genesis 1:2 tĕhôm) becomes as passable as dry land. The crossing reversed the chaos-order dynamic: what was impassable becomes passage.
10: "He saved them from the hand of the foe; he redeemed them from the hand of the enemy" — the military rescue language. "Redeemed (gāʾal)" — the kinsman-redeemer verb (also used in Psalms 69:18; Isaiah 43:1; Psalms 44:6) applied to the corporate rescue of Israel from Egypt. Exodus 15:13 uses the same verb ("in your unfailing love you will lead the people you have redeemed"). The sea crossing was not only miraculous escape but covenantal ransom — YHWH acted as Israel's gōʾēl, the close kinsman who buys back the enslaved family member. The "foe" (ʾōyēb) and "enemy (śônēʾ)" in parallel refer to the Egyptian army pursuing in chariots.
11: "The waters covered their adversaries; not one of them survived" — Exodus 14:28: "Not one of them survived." The totality of Egyptian military destruction at the sea is emphasized. The covering of the waters returns to the creation imagery of Genesis 1:2 (tĕhôm covering the deep) — the cosmic waters that YHWH held back for Israel's crossing now return to cover the Egyptians. The irony: the people who threw Israelite infants into the Nile (Exodus 1:22) are themselves swallowed by water. Psalms 78:53 draws the same connection. The complete annihilation of the pursuing army left Egypt militarily defenseless — a point emphasized in the Song of Moses.
12: "Then they believed his promises and sang his praise" — the brief moment of faith following the sea crossing. Exodus 14:31: "When the Israelites saw the mighty hand of the LORD displayed against the Egyptians, the people feared the LORD and put their trust in him and in Moses his servant." The Song of Moses immediately follows (Exodus 15:1-21). The psalm places this as a single verse between the great deliverance (Psalms 106:9-11) and the immediate onset of failure (Psalms 106:13-14) — the brevity is pointed: the faith lasted a very short time. The structure of the whole psalm is this pattern repeated: rescue, brief response, rapid return to failure.
13: "But they soon forgot what he had done and did not wait for his plan (ʿēṣāh) to unfold" — the transition word "but" (mĕhārû šāḵĕḥû = "they quickly forgot") marks the collapse of the faith of Psalms 106:12. In Exodus 15:22-17:7, the sequence from the sea crossing to the wilderness complaints is remarkably rapid — within days the people were complaining about water, food, and leadership. "Did not wait for his plan (ʿēṣāh)" — ʿēṣāh (counsel, plan, purpose) is the Wisdom vocabulary for the divine strategy operating through history (Proverbs 19:21; Isaiah 14:26-27; Psalms 46:10-11). They would not trust a plan they could not see.
14: "In the wilderness they gave in to their craving; in the wasteland they tested God (waynassû-ʾēl bîšîmôn)" — Num 11: the "lusting" (wĕyitʾawwû taʾăwāh — intense craving) for meat. The Israelites remembered the "free" fish, cucumbers, melons, leeks, onions, and garlic of Egypt (Numbers 11:5) and rejected the manna YHWH provided. "Tested God (nāsāh)" in the wilderness — the same verb as the Meribah place-name (Exodus 17:7 — Massah means "testing"). To test YHWH is to demand that he prove himself on their terms — the inversion of the proper relationship (YHWH tests people, not the reverse: Genesis 22:1; Deuteronomy 8:2).
15: "He gave them what they asked for, but sent a wasting disease among them" — Numbers 11:31-34: YHWH sent a wind that brought quail into the camp, but while the people were still eating, "the anger of the LORD burned against the people, and he struck them with a severe plague." The place was named Kibroth-hattaavah (Graves of Craving). The principle: God sometimes grants requests that are themselves a judgment — receiving what you demand when what you demand is against your good. Paul applies this pattern in 1 Corinthians 10:5-6: the wilderness events are recorded as "examples (tupoi) to keep us from setting our hearts on evil things as they did."
16: "In the camp they grew envious of Moses and of Aaron, the holy one of the LORD" — the Korah rebellion (Num 16). "Grew envious (wayyĕqannĕʾû)" — the same root as the "jealousy" / "zeal" vocabulary. Korah and his followers challenged Moses's and Aaron's authority: "The whole community is holy, every one of them, and the LORD is with them. Why then do you set yourselves above the LORD's assembly?" (Numbers 16:3). "Aaron, the holy one of the LORD (qĕdôš YHWH)" — this title appears elsewhere only in Psalms 16:10 (the "Holy One" whom God will not abandon to the grave) and is applied to Jesus in Mark 1:24. The priestly function is what is being challenged.
17: "The earth opened up and swallowed Dathan; it buried the company of Abiram" — Numbers 16:31-33: "the ground under them split apart and the earth opened its mouth and swallowed them and their households." Notably, the psalm names Dathan and Abiram (the Reubenite co-conspirators, Numbers 16:1) rather than Korah (the Levite who initiated the rebellion). Numbers 26:11 notes that "the sons of Korah, however, did not die" — Korah's line survived, and the sons of Korah later became a prominent musical guild associated with the Psalter (Pss 42-49; 84-85; 87-88). The selective naming may reflect sensitivity about this Korahite heritage.
18: "Fire blazed among their followers; a flame consumed the wicked" — Numbers 16:35: "And fire came out from the LORD and consumed the 250 men who were offering the incense." The 250 men who offered unauthorized incense were consumed by divine fire — the same judgment that fell on Nadab and Abihu (Leviticus 10:1-2) for offering "unauthorized fire" before YHWH. The bronze censers of the 250 were hammered into an overlay for the altar as a warning (Numbers 16:36-40). The twin judgments — earth swallowing (Korah, Dathan, Abiram) and fire consuming (the 250) — demonstrate YHWH's absolute authority over priestly access and appointment.
19: "At Horeb they made a calf and worshiped an idol of cast metal" — the golden calf (Exod 32). "Horeb" is Deuteronomy's name for Sinai — the psalm uses the northern/Deuteronomic terminology. The making of the calf while Moses was receiving the Torah on the mountain is the paradigmatic apostasy in the Hebrew Bible. The calf (ʿēgel) was likely a pedestal for the invisible YHWH (as the cherubim were in the tabernacle), but the prohibition of images was violated regardless of intent. Aaron's declaration "These are your gods, Israel, who brought you up out of Egypt" (Exodus 32:4) transferred the Exodus attribution from YHWH to the created object.
20: "They exchanged their glory (kĕbôdām) for an image of a bull that eats grass" — a devastating juxtaposition: the kābôd (glory, the presence of YHWH himself, the pillar of fire and cloud) for a grass-eating ox. Paul quotes this verse in Romans 1:23 as the paradigmatic act of human idolatry: "they exchanged the glory of the immortal God for images made to look like a mortal human being and birds and animals and reptiles." The exchange motif (give up X for Y) is Paul's diagnostic of universal religious failure — the same exchange that Israel made at Horeb is the structure of all idolatry. The bull eats grass — its nature is utterly mundane, incapable of the glory it supposedly represents.
21: "They forgot God their Savior (ʾēl môšîʿām), who had done great things in Egypt" — the golden calf episode is characterized as forgetting. Šākaḥ (forget) is the covenant anti-virtue, the opposite of zākar (remember) which Ps 105 repeatedly praised YHWH for doing. Deuteronomy 6:12; Psalms 8:14, 19 warn Israel three times "be careful that you do not forget the LORD your God." The saving acts in Egypt were so recent and so dramatic that the forgetting is presented as culpable — a willful suppression of evidence rather than a lapse of memory.
22: "Miracles in the land of Ham and awesome deeds by the Red Sea" — "land of Ham" (Egypt) as in Psalms 78:51; Psalms 105:23, 27. The "awesome deeds (nôrāʾôt)" by the Red Sea refer to both the sea crossing (Psalms 106:9-11) and the destruction of the Egyptians (Exodus 15:11: "Who among the gods is like you, LORD? Who is like you — majestic in holiness, awesome in glory, working wonders?"). The psalm lists the greatest demonstrations of divine power precisely at the point of greatest human ingratitude: the golden calf was made immediately after these events.
23: "So he said he would destroy them — had not Moses, his chosen one, stood in the breach before him to keep his wrath from destroying them" — Exodus 32:10-14: YHWH said "Now leave me alone so that my anger may burn against them and that I may destroy them" — and Moses interceded. "Stood in the breach (ʿāmaḏ bappeṣ)" — military imagery: standing in the gap in a wall to prevent enemy entry (cf. Ezekiel 22:30 — "I looked for someone among them who would build up the wall and stand before me in the gap on behalf of the land so I would not have to destroy it, but I found no one"). Moses's intercession is a type of mediation that prevents divine judgment — anticipating Christ's intercessory role.
24: "Then they despised the pleasant land (ʾereṣ ḥemdāh); they did not believe his promise" — the Kadesh-Barnea failure (Num 13-14). "Pleasant land (ʾereṣ ḥemdāh)" — the term suggests something desired, precious, delightful (Jeremiah 3:19; Zechariah 7:14 use the same phrase for the promised land). The people "despised" (wayyimʾăsû = rejected, scorned) the very thing YHWH promised — the failure to believe the spies' minority report (Caleb and Joshua) and the consequent refusal to enter. Hebrews 3:7-4:11 reads this episode as a warning to the church not to harden hearts as Israel did, so as not to fail to enter God's "rest."
25: "They grumbled in their tents and did not obey the LORD" — Numbers 14:1-4: the people wept aloud, grumbled against Moses and Aaron, and said "We should choose a leader and go back to Egypt." "Grumbled in their tents (wayyĕrāgĕnû bĕʾohŏlêhem)" — the image of private complaint circulating through the camp. The tent setting (private space) contrasts with the public announcement of YHWH's command — the refusal was whispered and spread before it became overt. Deuteronomy 1:27 recalls the same event: "You grumbled in your tents and said, 'The LORD hates us; so he brought us out of Egypt to deliver us into the hands of the Amorites to destroy us.'"
26: "So he swore to them with uplifted hand that he would make them fall in the wilderness" — the oath of judgment. In Numbers 14:28-35, YHWH swears (ḥay-ʾānî = as I live) that the generation that refused to enter will die in the wilderness over forty years — one year for each day of the spies' reconnaissance. "With uplifted hand" (wayyiśśāʾ yāḏô) — the standard gesture for oath-taking in the ANE, representing invocation of heaven as witness. Ezekiel 20:15, 23 also uses this gesture for divine oaths of judgment. The oath of blessing (Psalms 106:9 — sworn to Abraham) is now matched by an oath of judgment — both are equally irrevocable.
27: "...and make their descendants fall among the nations and scatter them throughout the lands" — the horizon of the oath extends beyond the wilderness generation to the exile. Leviticus 26:33-38; Deuteronomy 4:27; Psalms 28:64-68 all predict the scattering among the nations as the ultimate covenant curse for persistent unfaithfulness. The psalm, likely composed in or after the exile, reads the wilderness oath as having long-range consequences: the pattern of disobedience that began in the wilderness ultimately resulted in the destruction of Jerusalem and exile to Babylon. The psalm thus reads Israel's whole history as a single continuous pattern.
28: "They yoked themselves to the Baal of Peor and ate sacrifices offered to lifeless gods (zibḥê mētîm)" — the Baal of Peor episode (Numbers 25:1-9). Israel at Shittim joined in the Moabite sacred prostitution and sacrificial meals associated with Baal of Peor. "Yoked (wayyiṣṣāmĕdû)" — the image of being harnessed to, as animals are yoked to a plow: they attached themselves to the Baal. "Sacrifices offered to lifeless gods (mētîm)" — mētîm = the dead. The baals as dead things is the prophetic polemic against idols (cf. Psalms 115:4-8; Isaiah 44:9-20; Jeremiah 10:3-10). Paul quotes the Peor tradition in 1 Corinthians 10:8 as a warning: "We should not commit sexual immorality, as some of them did."
29: "They aroused the LORD's anger by their wicked deeds, and a plague broke out among them" — Numbers 25:3: "the LORD's anger burned against them" and Psalms 25:8-9: "the plague against the Israelites was stopped... but those who died in the plague numbered 24,000." The "plague" here is the divine response to covenant violation — the same mechanism as the plagues against Egypt, now turned inward against YHWH's own people. The covenant curses of Leviticus 26:25 and Deuteronomy 28:21-22 include pestilence as a consequence of idolatry. This episode demonstrates that the covenant's sanctions applied equally to Israel itself, not only to its enemies.
30: "But Phinehas stood up and intervened (wayyĕpalēl), and the plague was checked (wayyēʿăṣar hammaggēpāh)" — Numbers 25:7-8: Phinehas, grandson of Aaron, saw an Israelite man take a Midianite woman into his tent in full view of the congregation (while the people were weeping at the entrance to the tent of meeting), and drove a spear through both of them. "Intervened (pālaʾ)" — the verb means to pray/intercede (so most translations) or possibly to execute judgment. Either way, the act stopped the plague. This act of zealous intercession/judgment became paradigmatic in later tradition: 1 Macc Psalms 2:26 (Mattathias invokes Phinehas as the model for his killing of a Jew who approached a pagan altar); Sir Psalms 45:23-24 (Phinehas praised for making atonement for Israel).
31: "This was credited to him as righteousness for endless generations to come" — Numbers 25:12-13: YHWH gave Phinehas "my covenant of peace... a covenant of a lasting priesthood" because he was zealous for his God and made atonement for the Israelites. "Credited to him as righteousness (wattēḥāšeb lô lĕṣĕḏāqāh)" — the identical grammatical construction as Genesis 15:6: "Abram believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness." The parallel is deliberate: Abraham's faith and Phinehas's action are structurally equivalent in covenant-accounting — both are credited (ḥāšab) as righteousness by divine reckoning. Paul's argument in Rom 4 about Abraham's faith is thus part of a larger covenant-accounting tradition.
32: "By the waters of Meribah they angered the LORD, and trouble came to Moses because of them" — the waters of Meribah-Kadesh (Numbers 20:1-13, which is distinct from the earlier Meribah at Horeb in Exod 17). At Meribah-Kadesh, Moses and Aaron struck the rock twice instead of speaking to it as commanded, and YHWH said "Because you did not trust in me enough to honor me as holy in the sight of the Israelites, you will not bring this community into the land I give them" (Numbers 20:12). "Trouble came to Moses because of them (wayyēraaʿ lĕmōšeh biglĕlām)" — Moses suffered the consequences for his sin, but the people bore responsibility for provoking the situation by their rebellion.
33: "For they rebelled against the Spirit of God (ʾet-rûḥô), and rash words came from Moses' lips" — the causal chain: the people's rebellion provoked Moses to speak rashly. "They rebelled against his Spirit (mārû ʾet-rûḥô)" — Isaiah 63:10 uses the same vocabulary: "they rebelled and grieved his Holy Spirit." The Spirit of YHWH is grieved/rebelled against by Israel's persistent disobedience — a statement that anticipates Paul's warning in Ephesians 4:30 ("do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God"). Moses' rash words (bāṭāʾ biśpātāyw = spoke rashly with his lips) may refer to the angry address "Listen, you rebels" (Numbers 20:10) or to striking the rock as an act of angry deviation.
34: "They did not destroy the peoples as the LORD had commanded them" — the failure to drive out the Canaanites (Numbers 33:55; Judges 1:19-2:5). YHWH's command was explicit: drive out the inhabitants and destroy their religious sites (Exodus 34:11-16; Deuteronomy 7:1-5; Psalms 20:16-18). The reason given in the Torah texts is twofold: (1) the land must be ritually clean for YHWH's dwelling; (2) the Canaanites will ensnare Israel into idolatry if left. The actual situation in Judges shows the prophetic accuracy of this warning — the incomplete conquest immediately produced the syncretism the Torah warned against.
35: "But they mingled with the nations and adopted their customs (wayyilmĕdû maʿăśêhem)" — the Judges pattern described in summary. "Mingled (wayyitʿārbû)" — intermarried and socially integrated with Canaanites. "Adopted their customs (maʿăśêhem)" — learned and followed their practices. Deuteronomy 18:9 had warned "do not learn (lōʾ tilmad) to imitate the detestable ways of the nations." The covenant command "do not learn" is inverted in the psalm: "they learned (wayyilmĕdû)." The same word, the same command, the exact opposite outcome. This learning was the mechanism of assimilation — not forced but voluntary absorption into surrounding culture.
36: "They worshiped their idols, which became a snare (mōqēš) to them" — the Deuteronomic prediction fulfilled. Deuteronomy 7:16: "do not serve their gods, for that will be a snare (mōqēš) to you." The same word, the exact same outcome. The "snare" imagery: idolatry presents itself as attractive (hence the word "snare" — a trap that looks safe until it closes). Once Israel was ensnared in Canaanite worship, the difficulty of extraction was enormous — as the entire Judges-Kings narrative demonstrates.
37: "They sacrificed their sons and their daughters to false gods (laššēḏîm)" — the Molech worship and child sacrifice (Leviticus 18:21; Psalms 20:2-5; 2 Kings 23:10; Jeremiah 32:35). Šēḏîm (demons, false gods) — the term appears only here and in Deuteronomy 32:17 in this sense. The sacrifice of children is presented as the ultimate covenant violation — the complete inversion of the covenant promise of children as YHWH's gift (Psalms 127:3). Archaeological evidence for child sacrifice (molk offerings) in the Levant is debated but some evidence exists at Carthage (which colonized from Canaan) and in ancient Near Eastern texts.
38: "They shed innocent blood, the blood of their sons and daughters, whom they sacrificed to the idols of Canaan, and the land was desecrated by their blood" — the covenant-legal framing: bloodshed defiles the land (Genesis 4:10-12; Numbers 35:33-34: "Do not pollute the land where you are... bloodshed pollutes the land, and atonement cannot be made for the land on which blood has been shed"). The blood of sacrificed children "cried out" from the ground (the Cain and Abel principle — Genesis 4:10), and the land became ritually impure (ṭāmēʾ = defiled) through the accumulated bloodshed. The defilement of the land by innocent blood was one of the reasons given for YHWH's ejection of Israel from it (Ezekiel 22:1-16).
39: "They defiled themselves by what they did; by their deeds they prostituted themselves (wayyiznû bĕmaʿallêhem)" — "prostituted themselves" (zānāh) is the standard prophetic metaphor for idolatry as marital unfaithfulness (Hos 1-3; Jeremiah 3:1-9; Ezek 16; 23). Israel's covenant with YHWH was understood as a marriage — idolatry was adultery-and-prostitution. The metaphor was not merely rhetorical: the Baal cult explicitly involved sacred prostitution in some forms. The "defilement (ṭāmēʾ)" language links Psalms 106:39 with Psalms 106:38 — the same root for ritual impurity — connecting bloodshed and sexual apostasy as mutually reinforcing covenant violations.
40: "Therefore the LORD's anger burned against his people and he abhorred his inheritance" — the judicial conclusion to the catalogue of failures (Psalms 106:6-39). "Abhorred his inheritance (wĕyĕtaʿēb ʾet-naḥălātô)" — the language is startling: YHWH used the "abhor" word (tāʿab) that he applied to the Canaanite detestable practices (Leviticus 18:30; Deuteronomy 18:9-12) — now applied to Israel itself. The people who were told to avoid the abominations of the nations became abominable themselves. The covenant symmetry: the very language of covenant protection ("inheritance") is now paired with "abhorred."
41: "He gave them into the hands of the nations, and their enemies ruled over them" — the Judges cycle articulated in a single verse. The pattern of Judges 2:11-23 is exactly this: Israel abandons YHWH, YHWH "gives them into the hands of raiders," enemies rule, Israel cries out. "He gave them into the hands" (wayyittnēm bĕyad-gôyim) — the giving language is judicial: YHWH is the sovereign who decides the outcome of battles by handing over (or withholding) a nation. Israel's military defeats are interpreted theologically as YHWH's active decision to withdraw covenant protection.
42: "Their enemies oppressed them and subjected them to their power" — the experiential consequences of divine withdrawal: oppression (wayyaʿăšûm ʾôyĕbêhem) and subjugation. The root ʿāšaq (oppress, exploit) is the vocabulary of economic and social injustice — crushing those without power. The same word describes Israel's oppression in Egypt (Exodus 3:9) and the oppression the Torah laws were designed to prevent (Leviticus 19:13; Deuteronomy 24:14). In the irony of the covenant: Israel, rescued from oppression in Egypt, experienced the same oppression from the nations when they abandoned the covenant that was supposed to make them different from the oppressors.
43: "Many times he delivered them, but they were bent on rebellion (wĕhēmmāh yamrû bĕʿăṣātām) and were brought low in their sin" — the repeated-deliverance summary. "Many times (paʿamîm rabbôt)" covers the entire Judges cycle: Othniel, Ehud, Deborah, Gideon, Jephthah, Samson — and beyond into the Samuel-Kings period. "Bent on rebellion (yamrû bĕʿăṣātām)" — stubbornly, by their own counsel, they rebelled (Psalms 107:11 uses the same phrase). The pattern is not occasional failure but a structural orientation toward rebellion — a characteristic disposition that repeated deliverances failed to break. Paul reads this as evidence for the depth of human sinfulness requiring not repeated deliverance but fundamental transformation (Romans 7:14-25).
44: "But he took note of their distress when he heard their cry" — the pivot toward grace, after the long catalogue of failure. "Took note (wayyarĕʾ — he saw)" and "heard (wayišmaʿ)" — the same two verbs as Exodus 2:24-25 when YHWH responded to Israel's groaning in Egypt: "God heard their groaning... God saw the Israelites and was concerned about them." The repetition of the Exodus pattern signals that the covenant's rescue mechanism operates not because of Israel's merit but because YHWH sees and hears the distress of his covenant people. The cry of distress consistently activates the covenant regardless of the preceding failure.
45: "For their sake he remembered his covenant and out of his great love he relented (wayyinnāḥem kĕrōb ḥăsādāyw)" — the pivotal verse of the entire psalm. YHWH's motivation for continuing to deliver a persistently rebellious people: (1) covenant memory — the Abrahamic/Mosaic covenant still structures his relationship to Israel; (2) great steadfast love (rōb ḥăsādāyw = the abundance of his covenantal loyalties). "He relented (nāḥam)" — the same verb as Genesis 6:6 (YHWH "grieved/relented" that he had made humanity) and Exodus 32:14 (YHWH "relented" from the disaster he had threatened after Moses's intercession). Divine relenting is not weakness or inconstancy but the covenant loyalty overcoming judgment.
46: "He caused all who held them captive to show them mercy (wayyitten ʾôtām lĕraḥămîm liphĕnê kol-šôbêhem)" — a remarkable verse: YHWH changes the hearts of the captors toward mercy. This is precisely what happened in the Babylonian exile: Cyrus of Persia issued his decree of return (Ezra 1:1-4; Isaiah 44:28-45:1 prophesied it). YHWH moved the hearts of foreign rulers to show mercy to Israel. "Mercy (raḥămîm)" — the deep compassion/womb-love of Psalms 69:16 — applied here to what foreign rulers felt toward their captives. The mechanism: YHWH did not rescue Israel through military action but by softening the heart of those who held power over them.
47: "Save us, LORD our God, and gather us from the nations, to give thanks to your holy name and to glory in your praise" — the prayer that gives the whole psalm its purpose: the historical confession of failure (Psalms 106:6-46) was preparation for this petition. The three elements of the prayer: (1) "save us (hôšîʿēnû)" — the Exodus salvation verb applied to the present exile situation; (2) "gather us from the nations (qabĕṣēnû min-haggôyim)" — the ingathering from exile (Deuteronomy 30:3-5; Isaiah 43:5; Jeremiah 31:8-10); (3) the purpose: to give thanks and glorify. Restoration is not an end in itself but a means to renewed covenant worship. This verse places the psalm in the post-exilic period or in anticipation of exile.
48: "Praise be to the LORD, the God of Israel, from everlasting to everlasting. Let all the people say, 'Amen!' Praise the LORD (hallĕlû-yāh)!" — the doxology that closes Book IV of the Psalter (Pss 90-106). Each of the five books ends with a doxology (Psalms 41:13; Psalms 72:18-19; Psalms 89:52; Psalms 106:48; Psalms 150:6). "From everlasting to everlasting (mēhāʿôlām wĕʿaḏ-hāʿôlām)" — YHWH's existence spans both the remote past and the limitless future — his covenantal faithfulness is grounded in his eternal nature. "Let all the people say 'Amen!'" — the congregational response, the community ratifying the doxology as its own confession. The double Hallĕlû-yāh (closing this psalm and opening Ps 107, which begins Book V) creates a liturgical seam in the Psalter.
